displayTitle: 2026-06-24 - Health Committee - Briefing on mesh injury registries
description: 24 June 2026 | Health Committee Briefing on mesh injury registries Glenys Mahoney Drug Overdose (Assistance Protection) Legislation Bill DISC Trust Jason George, National Harm Reduction Lead Sam Allan Kahurangi Carter MP
descriptionSummary: Briefing on mesh injury registries. Glenys Mahoney. Drug Overdose (Assistance Protection) Legislation Bill

chunks.text: Here's some scones and gluten-free bulky things over there. Thank you. Good mor-... good afternoon, Glenys. Very good to have you here. I understand we had some challenges last time, but we're making it work now. So, look, welcome to the Health Committee. I'm Sam Atherton, chair of the Health Committee. We've got you here for 10 minutes to discuss your submission to us. If you want to leave us time for questions, you may. If you don't, that's up to you.

But I'll hand that time over to you now. Thank you. Thank you for the opportunity to speak today. My name is Glenys Mahoney. I have 20 years' surgical mesh lived experience, and am a consumer representative who has been part of the Surgical Mesh Credentialing and Registry work program for seven years.

I support the registry. I'm here today not to oppose it, but to ask a question I believe the committee needs to consider. Will the next generation of women receiving treatment for pelvic floor procedures be able to trust the registry mesh implantation records and long-term safety information in the years to come if it proceeds without legislative foundation?

The New Zealand Australasian Pelvic Floor Registry will record all pelvic floor procedures, mesh and non-mesh. It is not collecting data yet, but once it is running, it needs to accurately record all surgical mesh implanted detail, show the true rate of complications in New Zealand, and identify whether complications relate, reflect device problems, surgical technique, or broader system issues.

The key patient safety issue is that the registry cannot be reliable if every surgeon, private and public, participates and records the right information at the time of care. Mandatory participation is required. But in private practice, it relies on professional discretion rather than the same governance used in the public system.

Because missing data can't be added later, this creates a real risk that the registry may not have the complete, dependable information women need for safe care and quality of life in the years ahead. Right now, the systems are meant to ensure complete reporting are described as mandatory. But mandatory doesn't automatically mean safe or reliable.

We do not know if these systems will work every time, especially in private practice, or that they can prevent gaps in real time. A legislative registry is not only about clinicians reporting data. It's also about whether government agencies and regulatory bodies have clear legal responsibilities and can be held accountable if they fail to act.

There currently are non-legislative systems that support the registry, but the real issue is whether they're strong enough to protect women over time. Credentialing, the code of rights, funding contracts, accreditation standards, and the proposed Medical Products Bill. These all help, but each has limits, especially for privately funded surgeons, and none can guarantee complete, consistent reporting.

For women, this is not a problem with how the system is run. When data is missing, the consequences are physical and life-changing in their ability to work, to care for family, to take part in everyday life. Women want to get back to being fully functional members of their community after treatment.

That depends on a system that records what happens to them accurately every single time. So my question is this: Will these systems still hold in the future? Professional priorities shift. Governments change. Without legislation as a foundation, is there a risk these systems soften over time?

A system that relies on goodwill and professional norms without a legal floor is only as strong as the goodwill lasts. We have been here before. We trusted the system. Women were harmed. Now we're being asked to trust it again with no legal guarantee that the data will even be complete.

I have three requests, all centered on what women will need from the registry, not just now, but in the future, when many of today's decision-makers will have moved on and attentions may have shifted. My first request is for assurance about long-term consistency.

Women need confidence that every surgeon, private and public, will continue to provide complete, accurate data for every pelvic floor procedure year after year. The current system relies heavily on professional behavior, and we need clarity on how Health New Zealand and the Ministry of Health audits will ensure that full participation and correct documentation remain consistent over time My second request is about accountability, legally strong.

The systems we now have are helpful, but they're not law. So if a surgeon doesn't record a mesh implant properly, there's no legal requirement to make them do it. It's voluntary. Professional bodies, hospital processes, and government agencies all play a role, but none of them have clear legal duties to ensure complete reporting or to audit it consistently.

If the registry were backed by law, essential actions would become legal requirements, reporting complications, checking outcomes, follow up with patients, investigating safety issues, and auditing the data itself. That's what creates real, lasting accountability across the whole system, not just for individual clinicians.

In simple terms, professional systems rely on goodwill. Legal systems create obligations. And for women's safety, we need obligations that last. My final request is about patient confidence. Women need to know that the systems supporting the registry and the accountability behind them are strong enough to protect them, not just today, but decades from now.

Confidence is part of safety. For women who may live for years with the consequences of these procedures, the registry must be built on systems that are durable, enforceable, and capable of holding every surgeon and health system agency responsible for oversight, accountable for accurate documentation over the long term.

In 2016, this committee recommended a centralized surgical mesh registry. The government agreed. Nearly 10 years on, I am simply asking, is the registry we are building strong enough to last in private and public? The non-legislative systems could be used now.

But I am asking, do we need a legal foundation beneath them so the registry cannot be quietly narrowed or abandoned as priorities change over the years? I am a consumer, not a lawyer. I'm not here to tell the committee what the answer is. I am here to ask whether we are certain enough for the woman who will depend on this, that the answer is yes.

Women's trust in this registry will depend on whether accountability is real, durable, and enforceable, not just assumed through an administrative system. Their quality of life and treatment depends on it. Thank you. Thank you, Glenys. Thank you for your submission.

Do members have questions to raise? Dr. Verity. Thank you, Glenys. What would the consequence be if there was legislation saying this register had to exist and that the end date clinician did not put the information in the register?

I'm not really probably able to answer that. But obviously there needs to be processes within the appropriate channels, because it's very important for a woman to have that information if complications occur. Mm-hmm. Dr. Campbell. Excellent. Thank- We're running out of time.

Okay, I'll be very quick. Excellent. Thank you, Glenys. If we were going to summarize your request is that there is a registry in place, but you're concerned that because it's not in legislation that it's not going to have the same effect. We do have a number of registries which would probably fit into that same boat. Just off the top of my head, probably stroke, cardiac, the joint registry. There's probably others.

Why do you think that mesh in itself will be different to some of these other disease or clinical registries that we already have that are not legislated for? Because of the known harm that it's caused, and it's internationally not really known the extent of harm.

Glenys, can I just check that your previous answer to me is in part an answer to Dr. Campbell? Because one of the things is it's not just a set of clinical data, it's also like product tracing- Yes... so that someone can know what was done. Mm-hmm. Mind you, that would also be like the joint registry.

Correct. It's like two registries in one, because it's gonna record a product, but it's also gonna look at quality and safety of care. And outcomes, I assume. Yes. All right. Thank you, Glenys. Thank you for making yourself available to the committee and for your submission and for taking some questions. We greatly appreciate it. Thank you.
We'll move back to...
Is this in person or Zoom? Zoom. Oh, sorry. Hmm.

Hello, Jason. Very good to have you here from the DISC Trust. I'm Sam Affendich, chair of the committee. Just to make you aware, the rules of Parliament mean that when hearing your submission, we have to be careful not to hear allegations that may seriously damage a person's reputation. Generally, please don't name other people, make serious allegations against named or identifiable people, or include specific or detailed information about the circumstances of an overdose.

On that note, we have you here for 10 minutes. If you want to leave us time for questions, you may. I will hand that time over to you now. Thank you. Thank you. Tena koutou katoa. My name is Jason George, and I'm appearing on behalf of DISC Trust, where I am the national harm reduction lead.

I've worked in harm reduction for a little over 20 years, including alongside people who inject and use drugs in overdose prevention, naloxone access, drug checking, and various other harm reduction measures. DISC Trust strongly supports the Drug Overdose Assistance Protection Legislation Bill.

The central purpose is simple. When someone may be overdosing, people should seek emergency help immediately without fear that doing so will expose them or others to avoidable legal consequences. Laws of this kind are an important harm reduction measure to reduce preventable overdose deaths.

Comparable Good Samaritan overdose laws are now in place in every state of the United States as well as in Canada. They recognize a basic public health principle. In a possible overdose, preserving life must come before fear of punishment. This bill gives New Zealand the opportunity to put that same principle clearly into law.

I want to explain why this bill matters from a firsthand overdose response experience. Some years ago, a friend experienced an overdose situation at their flat in the same residential complex where I lived. In a panic, rather than immediately calling 111, they came approximately two minutes away to my place, banged on the door, and in a very panicked state, asked for help.

I hurried to the flat with them and assessed the situation and concluded that emergency medical assistance was going to be needed. This was a serious overdose. The person was clearly struggling to breathe, and it wasn't a situation that could be left.
It was before naloxone was widely available in New Zealand.

So there was a discussion about whether to call 111 immediately or to move the person to outside to a car park, to some location away from the flat, but where assistance could be provided without fear of the person whose residence it was getting in trouble with the police for drugs that were there, utensils, and a small number of cannabis plants.

I called 111. It was eventually agreed that 111 would be called, and the ambulance would be called to the flat without moving the person. But time was wasted while this was debated. I called 111 and began CPR. While I was doing that, other person or people at the scene cleaned up drugs, paraphernalia, hid the three or four cannabis plants that were there in wardrobes, and removed drugs from the person who had overdosed, all because they were worried about legal consequences.

Those actions were understandable in the context of fear of prosecution, but they were not the actions needed in the moment. The person needed everyone's attention and assistance. It was very difficult managing an overdose sort of that overdose assistance with a larger person essentially solo while everyone else cleaned up the scene.

Eventually, after what seemed like an eternity, ambulance staff arrived and took over, administered multiple doses of naloxone and eventually oxygen, and transported the person to hospital. They survived, but critical time had been lost, and it was a fortunate and very lucky outcome that could have easily gone differently.

That experience made a lasting impression on me. The danger was not only the overdose itself. The danger was also the hesitation, uncertainty, and fear of police involvement that diverted people from seeking help and assisting the person in front of them. My experience with this experience is not isolated. Over many years working alongside peers and people who use drugs, I have heard repeated accounts of people being moved into car parks, taken to phone boxes, or simply left and dumped during overdose situations because those present feared arrest or police

involvement. Those actions can turn an already dangerous overdose into a fatal one Moving an unconscious person can delay care and create further risk. Leaving someone alone means there may be nobody to call for health, administer naloxone, provide CPR, or tell responders what they may have taken. These are not decisions people make because they do not care.

They are decisions made in panic, fear, and uncertainty about what will happen if the police become involved. This is why the bill matters. It should create enough certainty that when someone may be dying, people call 111 immediately, stay with them, give first aid or naloxone where they can, if necessary, and provide responders with accurate information. Nobody should be deciding whether to move an unresponsive person, hide drugs, remove paraphernalia, conceal plants, or leave someone alone before calling for help.

This bill is not blanket immunity. It is not a license for serious offending, exploitation, violence, or organized commercial drug dealing. It is a targeted public health measure designed to remove a barrier to life-saving assistance in an emergency. DISC's submission calls for strong and unambiguous protections.

In support of that position, I encourage the committee to consider two practical refinements. First, the protections should clearly extend to non-commercial supply agreements, including social sharing, as outlined in the bill. In real life, drugs are not always obtained or distributed in a legally neat way. Friends may pool money, purchase together, share a substance, ask someone else to obtain it for them, or have one person obtain drugs and distribute them among a small group without making a profit.

These situations can fall outside a narrow understanding of social sharing, even though they are not commercial dealing. A person should not hesitate to call an ambulance because they fear that obtaining drugs for friends, passing drugs on, or being connected to how the drugs were obtained will automatically be treated as supply outside the protection.

The distinction should also be whether there is evidence of commercial dealing, profit-making, exploitation, or organized distribution. Where there is no such evidence, non-commercial supply arrangements should be explicitly protected. Second, the committee should review the two-plant cultivation threshold. In the situation I have described, plants were hidden because people feared consequences of them being seen. A very low threshold may leave non-commercial growers uncertain about whether the protection applies.

In this case, there were more plants, about three or four, I believe, that would have fallen outside the protection offered. But there was no commercial element to those plants being grown. Passing the bill is essential, but people also need to know that the protection exists. DISC supports clear public information for people who use drugs, whanau, peers, emergency services, police, health providers, and community organizations.

The message should be simple: someone may be overdosing, call 111, stay with them, provide assistance if you can, and tell responders what they need to know. DISC also supports monitoring and evaluations so the impact of the legislation on emergency help-seeking, overdose outcomes, and engagements with services can be understood over time.

DISC Trust urges the committee to recommend that the bill proceed. It is a practical and humane measure that places preservation of life above punitive responses in a medical emergency. The key test is simple: when someone may be overdosing, does the law make it easier for people to call for help immediately? Strong, clear, and workable protections will do that.

I welcome any questions, and thank you for your time. Thank you, Jason. We have just over a minute left for questions. Very good submission, thank you. And up to members, Ingrid Leary. Yeah, thanks for your submission. Totally understand why you are making the distinction between personal use and commercial use. If we think about the person who might be on the premises purchasing from a dealer, who then suffers a medical event, they're going to be less protected. Do you think practically what will happen is dealers will no longer enable that on their premises, or how are you going to

safeguard those people from not being excluded by this protection? Well, I think there is a risk that, as it's written, that they will be excluded. I think perhaps the committee could consider whether there's a way of implementing in the law something around the scale of whether there's evidence of a sort of commercial scale operation or whether it's a lower sort of level supply.

Perhaps without any firm evidence of that, the protections should probably apply. All right. Thank you, Jason. We are at time. Appreciate your time and making yourself available for us, for the committee today. Kia ora. Thank you. All right.

We will move on to our next submitter, which is Sam Allen. Sam, welcome to the Health Committee. Just to read you some rules that we have. The rules of Parliament mean that when you're hearing your submission, we have to be careful not to hear allegations that may seriously damage a person's reputation. Generally, please don't name other people, make serious allegations against named or identifiable people, or include specific or detailed information about the circumstances of an overdose. We have you here for five minutes.

If you want to leave us time for questions, you may. I'll hand that time over to you now. Thank you Awesome. Kia ora koutou. Ko Sam toku ingoa. No Ōtutahi ahau. So what I'm going to do is firstly, I'm going to briefly touch on my submission, and then I'll hopefully leave multiple minutes for time, and if there isn't people with questions, I'll extend on my submission.

What my submission tried to explain is coming from the perspective in both an unpartisan way, but also for someone that doesn't drink or do drugs, is that prosecution does not happen regardless of this legislation, and therefore, the only risk is people's perception of a risk of prosecution. And therefore, the only impacts which accrue are those to health, and therefore this legislation should pass.

I think what is important here is that we take the nuance out of people's thoughts. The message can be clear, it can be simple, and that is just to ring 111. I think the time that people pause to think is incredibly important, right? If you're someone that has taken these substances or you're just incredibly worried about your friends, people might think about whether they're betraying them.

They just perceive the risk to be very high because they are not sober, and therefore the law is able to give them certainty that they don't need to, in a panic, make these decisions. And I think we're starting to see the kind of benefits of this certainty come through at university, which I currently study at. Even though I don't use drugs, people that do are starting to do things like get them checked. And instead of worrying about, "Oh, am I able to do this in a safe way?" Because the law is clear, people are willing to engage in that. I've got a wee bit more to

extend on, but I'm happy to take questions before then, because there's fully time. All right. All right. I'll ask a question then. Chung, Chung. Just now you talk about the perception and then people taking drugs shouldn't be worried about the safety stuff. I think my message is, you shouldn't be the main messages about stopping people taking drugs at the beginning. Rather than they should be thinking about, "Oh yeah, it's safe to take drugs.

It's safe to overdose." Don't you think it's a wrong messages to our young people? I think that's completely inaccurate to the way that this message is likely to be portrayed because if you had a magic solution to stop people from taking drugs, I think you'd probably be a very popular parliamentarian if you were able to be the person that ended people consuming drugs. But obviously this isn't the case, right?

People get addicted, people engage socially. We've had the Misuse of Drugs Act since the 1980s, and people still do it. What I think is important here is that the people that do engage in this message is just the idea of ringing 111. It isn't to say drugs are safe because obviously if you're considering an overdose, it is very unsafe. So I think that kind of messaging would go to say that potentially if we just didn't let people seek help, that would be good because it would mean that therefore we'd send this really clear message to not take drugs.

I think we just need to reckon with the reality that currently people take drugs when we have the kind of messages you support. I think that it is unlikely people see the potential for an ambulance to be called faster and think that it's fine. I think that the other submissions explain that people do worry about this, and as my submission explains, by the time an overdose has occurred, people have already made the decision to take drugs, and I think to not support this bill, you would therefore need to explain why, prior to people taking the drugs,

people then considered the ambulance availability, which I think is incredibly speculative to believe. All right. Do other members have questions? No, but I do just want to acknowledge Sam for your submission. Kia ora. Good to see you again.

Good to see you as well. And thank you for engaging in the process. Sam was Hamish's- Youth MP... Youth MP. Excellent. All right, Sam. Hey, thank you very much for your submission and for turning up here and submitting in front of us today. Awesome. Ngā mihi koutou.
Thank you.

Who do we have now? Kahu Rangi Carter, MP. Yeah. Round two. How's our technology going? It's tight today. Great. Excellent. Hey, Kahu, good to have you up on stage. We've got you here for 15 minutes. You've already heard the rules around sensitive personal information, so I won't read those to you again.

If you want to leave us time for questions, of course you may. You have 15 minutes. Thank you. Thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you to the committee for having me here and to all of the people who have taken the time to submit on this bill. Today, I'm speaking to my member's bill, the Drug Overdose Assistance Protection Legislation, commonly known around the world as the Good Samaritan Law, because it protects people who call for help when someone is in need of life-saving medical assistance.

It takes a harm reduction approach to drug overdoses and drug use, and we want to remove any barriers to people accessing life-saving care and further support because no one should ever die of a drug overdose. We want people to call for help even when they are in doubt.

Before we look into the details of my Good Samaritan Law, I would like to thank the members of the Health Select Committee for your interest in the law and acknowledge how the member's bill process has worked so well this term. We have seen members' bills from all across the house proceed through the first reading, like my colleague, and Deputy Speaker, Teanau Tuiono's Samoan citizenship bill passing earlier this term. And having a bill drawn from the biscuit tin is exciting for staff and MPs, and I want to acknowledge all the MPs

and staff who have diligently and inquisitively worked to strengthen and understand laws that can help the people of Aotearoa, New Zealand. This bill, the Good Samaritan Bill, was pulled just a couple of weeks after being put in the biscuit tin. Some might say we've got some guardian angels blessing this bill.

As a first-term MP, it's been encouraging and really cool to see strong democracy in real time, where politicians work together to make laws that make our country safer for everyone. The Good Samaritan laws around the world introduce legal protections for people who assist someone in the event of a drug overdose or serious unexpected reaction to drugs.

This law would make a narrow change to the Misuse of Drugs Act 1975, that sits with the Ministry of Health, the Bail Act 2000, and the Parole Act 2002 to ensure that when someone calls for life-saving help during a suspected drug overdose or serious adverse reaction, they will not be charged for specified low-level drug offenses like having or using drugs for personal use, having drug utensils, enabling premises for low-level offending or

non-commercial social supply, breaching parole conditions relating to drug use, recognizing the heightened overdose risk after leaving prison. I want to make it clear to every member and the public, this bill does not protect dealing or serious crime. It is about ensuring that if someone needs medical assistance, they get that help.

Coroner's findings have found over and over again that hesitation to call for help driven by the fear of getting in trouble with the law is a contributing factor in fatal overdoses. We want to unequivocally make it clear when someone is in need of life-saving care, that they will not be in trouble with the law, and the person calling for help will not be.

In 2025, the New Zealand Drug Foundation survey found that 39% of people at risk of drug harm would be worried to call 111 for help if someone was experiencing a drug overdose. 39%. And a recent Victoria University survey found that over 50% of students knew someone who had overdosed on drugs.

I'm a mum, and my kids are the most precious things in the world to me. My kids are 17 and 18, nearly 18 and 19, and my daughter is planning to go away to uni next year. If they or someone they know was ever in a situation like this, I would want any of the barriers to that life-saving care to be removed, and that is what this bill is aiming to do.

We know drug harm is increasing in Aotearoa, and there are scary toxic drugs hitting our shores, and we have to make sensible drug laws to reduce harm and deaths from overdoses. People often take drugs without knowing it has dangerous additives or is something different to what they expected. And I want to remind all of the members that it may be the first time somebody ever tries a drug that an overdose occurs.

I first learned that people were dying in New Zealand unnecessarily to treatable, avoidable drug overdoses when I read the recommendations from the New Zealand Drug Foundation, and I want to mahi to them for this mahi. And their recommendations had the aim of reducing drug harm in our communities with sensible laws.

I was shocked to read that almost three people a week, yes, three people a week die of treatable accidental drug overdoses in Aotearoa, New Zealand. I was floored and wanted to learn more about it, and it became clear that the Good Samaritan law was common sense, and because similar laws overseas have proven to reduce harm, I thought that this would be an awesome member's bill. It's not often we get the chance to save lives with our member's bills.

Evidence and data supports it, and I know that it can be supported across the house. When I spoke to mothers and whanau of people who had lost children to treatable drug overdoses, I knew it was something that I wanted to shepherd through to law.

Arriving to a fatal overdose is horrific for police, other first responders, the people at the scene And the loss and grief will be felt by families and friends forever. This law can prevent those tragedies from ever happening. The most effective versions of Good Samaritan laws are those that protect the person experienced, the overdose, as well as the people who are on the scene.

I know that many of you here today are parents, and though we might not agree on what drugs should be legal, I hope we can all agree that if someone is overdosing on drugs, that they should get critical life-saving care. There's no typical drug user in New Zealand. People of all ages from all backgrounds and regions take drugs. This law follows similar laws internationally, like Canada and Australia, which have reduced deaths by almost 10% over a couple of years, and if replicated in New Zealand, could save up to 19

to 29 lives per year. And that is what we are doing here today. That is what this bill is about. Overdoses don't wait for a moral argument about whether people should take drugs or not. We must remove barriers to people getting life-saving medical care. Thank you, Mr. Chair.

Thank you, Kahurangi. We have time for questions. Dr. Verall. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thanks for your presentation, Kahu. Do you support the Drug Foundation's recommendations for changes to the bill? There is a lot more that we could do with this bill.

It is really important that we, as a committee, go through the recommendations from the Drug Foundation, from the Law Commission, and get advice from our advisors, from the Ministry of Health, because we want to make this law as good as possible so that we do save those lives.

Ingrid. I just wonder if you could talk through the fact that we have cases in New Zealand where people have experienced effective overdose on alcohol. What is the difference as you see it between that kind of situation and another? We know that the number one cause of deaths from drugs is due to alcohol in New Zealand.

And we want to make sure that we have sensible alcohol reform laws as well as other laws like the Good Samaritan law that can make sure that people unequivocally know that they can call for help. Sorry. I probably wasn't clear in my question.

If you've got two cases- Mm... under the law as it currently stands- Mm-hmm... somebody who has imbibed alcohol and effectively overdoses on it- Thank you... would probably not have reluctance to call services. How does that sit with you given the alternative drugs and what scenarios play out there?

Thank you for clarifying the question. If people need life-saving care, they should get it. That is what this bill is about. And in most instances where people call for life-saving care or save a life, they're considered a hero, whether it's helping somebody who is having a terrible reaction to alcohol poisoning or saving somebody who is drowning.

They're considered a hero, and we want that to be the case for this law here. And I want to remind all the members that this is a narrow law change. It's not going to save the world, but it might save some lives, and I think that is worthwhile, and I really do appreciate everybody taking the time to really dig into this law to make sure that it is fit for purpose as it goes out into the public.

Thank you. Carlos. When you keep mentioning this law is happening internationally, which country are you refer to? So we have the USA, Australia, Canada, many countries, and like previous submitters have said, there's many comparable countries who have Good Samaritan laws.

Because actually, I do my research. The only one actually have the real legislation is Canada. Even US, there are only certain state have the law, not every single state in US. And other country you refer to, it's only taking a part of it, not actually setting the legislation. Are you agree with that? What we do know, and thank you for doing that research and looking into it.

What we do know is that countries that have Good Samaritan laws that protect the person experiencing the overdose and those calling for help and on the scene are the most effective. And the reason, there's many reasons, but the key thing here is that at the moment, people are not calling for help when someone needs life-saving care. That is what we are doing here, and that's what this legislation is wanting to do.

So do you agree with me that this law is not actually a legislation in some of the country you mentioned? It is, and we can send that to you- Only Canada and US... at a later date. I can send it to you afterwards. All right. And also, these are great questions to have for our advisors who will be able to dig into that data.

I have done written parliamentary questions to, for example, the Minister for Police, but they were not able to provide some of that data that I was looking for. So it'll be great to get those officials in. One more question. Can you confirm that the only country actually having the similar legislation, which is Canada and US, are the coming number one and two in worldwide for drug being use?

For what, sorry? For drug usage. For illegal drugs usage, they're coming first and second in the world. Can you confirm that? I can't confirm that right now, and this is- I can tell you they are... about- Based on the report... this is about- Yes, they are I can see you're, you're making a moral- Point of order, Mr. Speaker. Point of order. Point of order, Hon Clare Curran.

Dr. Chung's questions are based on some assumptions, and I get that he's- Sorry, it's not a, assumption on the report . Yeah, I- Speaking... but we haven't clarified the situation. I think the member has answered that question, and then there's a follow-up question that assumes the veracity of the information that Dr. Chung gave. It might be a better question to come after we've had official advice.

Thank you. And- Speaking to the point of order. Point of order, Carl Leong. I think this is not assumption. There's public release. There's a lot of research. US and Canada actually are coming first and second. I think the member of the opposite, they should do their homework before they actually comment that I make assumption. Uh, I can see that this is a topic that we're all really passionate about, and I want to remind the members that this, we can have a moral argument about which country takes the most drugs and why.

What this legislation is doing is ensuring that our young people, that anybody across Aotearoa who needs life-saving care, is not scared to call for help. We can have a conversation about- Yeah, sure... the morals of further things later. But in this legislation, we want to make sure that this narrow law change, which could save lives and was brought to Parliament by moms, and whanau, and people with lived experience who have lived with the heartache and pain of losing a loved one,

have the time here to be heard by our lawmakers, by us- Thank you, Kahurangi... and make a law that works for the people of Aotearoa. Thank you, Kahurangi. I'm sure we can explore a number of those points as we go on. Like, I think- Thank you... for members, if they say things in here, we assume them to be honorable and take them at their word.
So- Thank you so much... thank you very much for your submission and presentation to the committee today. Appreciate it. Okay. We'll move back to private session
